model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DEPTH = 2","new":"DEPTH = 3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.05","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 48","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Increase MLP capacity modestly and add a small amount of dropout. The baseline network is very small for CIFAR-10, so widening and slightly deepening it is the highest-leverage simple change.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DEPTH = 3","new":"DEPTH = 2","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.05","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 5e-4","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.999)","new":"ADAM_BETAS = (0.9, 0.95)","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The widened 3-layer version underperformed, so this step simplifies the MLP back toward a cleaner 2-layer shape and removes dropout. At the same time, switching to AdamW with a slightly higher learning rate and lighter beta damping should help optimization under the fixed step budget.","secondary_modes":["indexing","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 320","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"layers.append(nn.GELU())","new":"layers.append(nn.ReLU())","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Keep the AdamW setup that helped, then make the MLP a bit wider and switch to ReLU, which is a more standard pairing with BatchNorm for this kind of flat CIFAR-10 MLP. The prior step suggested the bigger issue was model/activation fit rather than optimizer family alone.","secondary_modes":["indexing","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 7e-4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The schedule change helped, so the next low-risk move is to reduce the base step size slightly. With cosine decay already active, a smaller initial learning rate should cut overshoot and improve validation loss under the fixed-step budget.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 7e-4","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Restore the better step size and increase batch size so each fixed-budget update uses more data. On this MLP, the current lower learning rate appears to underfit, and the larger batch should make the training signal cleaner without changing the overall structure.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 128","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"Push the batch size once more to exploit the fixed step budget with more data per update. The last step showed a large gain from a bigger batch, so this is the lowest-risk continuation of that trend.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The fixed-step regime is now working well, so the next likely bottleneck is over-regularization rather than optimization. Lowering weight decay should let the MLP fit a bit more while keeping the same batch size, schedule, and optimizer.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 256","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 384","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The optimization setup is now stable, and the last change produced essentially no gain, which suggests the model may be capacity-limited. Widening the two hidden layers is the simplest next architecture move with a reasonable chance of improving validation loss.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 384","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The larger two-layer MLP is still a plausible bottleneck, and the last widening step helped. Pushing the hidden width once more keeps the stable optimization setup intact while testing whether the model is still under-capacity.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 768","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current setup is still improving, but the gains are small enough that extra width is the most plausible remaining lever. Raising the first hidden size keeps the same optimizer and schedule while testing whether the MLP is still slightly under-capacity.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 768","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The wider 768-unit variant regressed, so the safest move is to return to the best-performing width and relax decay a bit further. That keeps the stable optimizer/schedule setup while testing whether the current plateau is still slightly over-regularized.","secondary_modes":["layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-5","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 0.0","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The last reduction in weight decay produced a tiny gain, which suggests the current setup is still slightly over-regularized. Dropping decay to zero is the next simple test of that hypothesis without changing the optimizer or capacity.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 8e-4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The current run is flat, so the remaining lever worth testing is a slightly smaller base step size for finer convergence. With the fixed cosine schedule already in place, this is the simplest way to probe whether the model is bouncing around a shallow optimum.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DEPTH = 2","new":"DEPTH = 3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 8e-4","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The lower learning rate did not help, so restore the proven step size and use the remaining budget to test more model capacity. A third hidden layer at the same stable optimizer settings is the most plausible way to escape the current plateau.","secondary_modes":["topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DEPTH = 3","new":"DEPTH = 4","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"Depth was the strongest recent signal: moving from two to three hidden layers produced a clear improvement. Testing one more layer is the most direct way to extend that trend while keeping the optimizer, batch size, and schedule unchanged.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DEPTH = 4","new":"DEPTH = 3","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The depth-4 variant regressed, and the best visible result came from depth 3 with the same optimizer and schedule. Reverting to that shallower shape is the most direct way to recover the better-performing architecture without adding any new moving parts.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 768","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The depth-3 model has matched the best observed loss, so the remaining obvious axis is width. The earlier 768-unit attempt was on a shallower network, so this tests whether a wider first hidden layer helps once the deeper stack is already in place.","secondary_modes":[]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 768","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 640","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.02","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The 768-wide variant looks a bit too large for this depth-3 MLP, so this step pulls width back toward the apparent sweet spot and adds a small amount of dropout. The goal is to keep most of the extra capacity from the deeper model while trimming the over-capacity that showed up in the last run.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.02","new":"DROPOUT_RATE = 0.0","anchor":"","text":"","source":""},{"op":"replace_exact","function":"","old":"FC_HIDDEN = 640","new":"FC_HIDDEN = 512","anchor":"","text":"","source":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The last widening plus dropout step regressed, so the most reliable final move is to return to the best-known configuration. This restores the depth-3, 512-wide, no-dropout MLP that already produced the strongest verified loss in the run.","secondary_modes":["caching"]}
